figures.description: The top part shows three example ARC tasks, each with a few input-output pairs (x, y) for training and one input (x_infer) for testing. The bottom part illustrates the Vision ARC (VARC) framework, showing how training tasks (x, y) and test tasks (x_infer) are processed by a VARC network to produce model predictions (y_infer). The examples demonstrate concepts like reflection, symmetry, and gravity, with visual grids of colored squares.

This figure displays three unseen test tasks solved by VARC. Each panel shows a task with demonstration input-output pairs (x, y) at the top and the model's prediction (y_infer) for a given test input (x_infer) at the bottom. The tasks involve transforming grids of colored squares, demonstrating VARC's ability to generalize to new, challenging visual reasoning problems. For example, one task involves filling a shape, another involves mirroring, and a third involves a complex pattern transformation.

This figure illustrates the ARC problem definition. It shows a training set (T_train) with two example tasks (Task 1 and Task 400), each having demonstration pairs (x, y) and inference inputs (x_infer). It also shows a test set (T_test) with two new tasks (Task 1 and Task 400), similarly structured. The model is expected to infer the output (y_infer) for the given x_infer in both training and test tasks, using the provided demonstration pairs. The inference outputs are marked with question marks, indicating the model's prediction target.

This figure demonstrates the scale and translation transformations applied to a raw input grid before placing it on a 'canvas'. The left side shows a raw input grid being scaled up (duplicated pixels) to a larger size. The right side shows the scaled grid being randomly translated and placed onto a fixed-size gray canvas, ensuring all pixels are visible. These transformations are used as data augmentations.

This figure illustrates the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture used in VARC. The input, which is a grid randomly placed on a canvas, is first processed by a patch embedding layer. The embedded patches, along with a task token, are then fed into a stack of Transformer blocks. Finally, a predictor layer generates the output, which is the model's prediction for the transformed grid. The 'off canvas' area is also shown, indicating the background color.

Two pairs of input/output grids demonstrating shape handling. The left pair shows a 1x scaling ratio, with a small input grid and a slightly larger output grid. The right pair shows a 2x scaling ratio, with a larger input grid and an even larger output grid. In both cases, gray pixels denote background tokens, and white pixels denote border tokens indicating the output shape.

A line graph showing the evaluation accuracy (%) on the ARC-1 eval set as a function of the number of offline training RE-ARC pairs per task (log-scaled). Accuracy increases from around 30% with 0 pairs to over 50% with 1000 pairs, showing diminishing returns.

A line graph showing the evaluation accuracy (%) on the ARC-1 eval set as a function of offline training task diversity (log-scaled). Accuracy increases from around 25% with 0 tasks to over 50% with 400 tasks, indicating that diversity is beneficial.

Four line graphs showing Pass@k (Cumulative Success Rate) on ARC-1 (left) and ARC-2 (right) evaluation sets, for both a single ViT-18M model (top) and an ensemble (bottom). In all graphs, Pass@k increases with k, showing that more proposals lead to higher success rates. The ensemble generally achieves higher Pass@k values than the single model.

A grid of successful and failed examples from ARC-1. Each example shows an input grid, a ground truth output grid, and the model's predicted output grid. Successful examples show the model's prediction matching the ground truth, while failed examples show discrepancies.
A grid of successful and failed examples from ARC-2. Similar to Figure 17, each example shows input, ground truth, and predicted output grids, highlighting both correct and incorrect predictions.

An example of an ambiguous ARC task showing an input grid, a ground truth output grid, and two plausible solutions generated by the model. The first solution interprets the rule as 'turn red box blue only if extended blue lines go through the box,' while the second interprets it as 'turn red box blue if extended blue lines touch the box in any form.'
A visualization of pixel-wise attention maps. For a specified query pixel, the map shows the attention weights across the input grid, highlighting which input regions the model focuses on.

A visualization of layer-wise attention maps. For different layers of the model, the attention maps averaged across all pixels are shown, indicating the overall focus of the model at different processing stages.

A visualization of test-time training evolution. Each row represents a distinct test task, showing the model's predicted output grid at different stages of test-time training, demonstrating how predictions are progressively refined.

tables.description: Compares configurations for offline training and test-time training, detailing epochs (100 for both), warmup epochs (10 for both), optimizer (Adam for both), batch size (32 for offline, 8 for test-time), learning rate (3e-4 for both), learning rate scheduler (cosine for both), weight decay (0 for both), and dropout (0.1 for both).

Reports the running time of the ViT-18M model for both offline and test-time training. Offline training uses 8 H100 GPUs for 4.8 hours, while test-time training uses 1 H100 GPU for 0.7 seconds per epoch.

Details the configurations for three ViT architectures (6M, 18M, 66M), specifying hidden dimension (384, 512, 768), Transformer blocks (5, 10, 20), number of heads (8, 8, 12), MLP block hidden dimension (512 for all), dropout (0.1 for all), patch size (2x2 for all), and canvas size (64x64 for all).

Details the configurations for three U-Net architectures (7M, 17M, 55M), specifying the number of stages (3 for all), layers per stage (1, 1, 2), channels at resolution 1 (80, 120, 160), attention at resolution 1 (No for all), channels at resolution 2 (160, 240, 320), attention at resolution 2 (Yes for all), channels at resolution 3 (160, 240, 320), attention at resolution 3 (Yes for all), and mid block (No for all).
